1: zevach shelamim (H2077+H8002): 'fellowship offering' (also rendered 'peace offering,' 'well-being offering'). Shelem derives from shalom (wholeness, completeness, peace) or from shalem (to complete, to repay, to be at peace). The shelamim is the only offering from which the offerer also eats — after YHWH's portion (the fat burned on the altar) and the priestly portion, the offerer and family share a communal meal. It is a meal of reconciled community between the worshiper, the priests, and YHWH. Tamim (H8549): without defect — applies to both male and female animals in the shelamim, unlike the 'olah which required male only.
2: vesomach yado al rosh qorbano: laying of the hand on the offering's head — the semikhah gesture (covered in depth at 1:4). Shachat: slaughter. Zaraq dam: throw/toss blood. The same three acts (hand-laying, slaughter, blood-sprinkling) that characterize the 'olah initiate the shelamim as well. The shared ritual structure across offering types signals that they participate in the same fundamental logic of substitution and approach, even as the disposition of the animal differs.
3: chelev (H2459): 'fat' — specifically the superior, internal fat covering the organs (as distinct from the ordinary fat of muscle). The chelev belongs exclusively to YHWH: 3:16 will state kol chelev la-YHWH ('all fat is for YHWH'), and 3:17 forbids any Israelite from eating it. The best fat as YHWH's portion communicates that the highest and richest part of the animal belongs to the giver of all life. The same logic governs firstfruits (agricultural best to YHWH) and firstborn (progeny-best to YHWH).
4: kelayot (H3629): 'kidneys' — in Hebrew thought the kidneys (like the heart) are associated with the moral and emotional interior of a person (kelayot va-lev = 'kidneys and heart' = the deepest self, e.g. Ps 16:7; 73:21; Jer 11:20). Offering the kidneys to YHWH symbolizes offering the hidden interior. Yoteret al ha-kaved: the fatty lobe or appendage of the liver. The liver was associated in ANE divination with revealing divine will (haruspicy). Offering this organ to YHWH implicitly rejects human divination: the 'divine indicator' is burned up as YHWH's portion, not inspected by humans.
5: al ha-'olah: burned 'on top of the burnt offering' on the altar. The shelamim's fat portions are added to the already-burning 'olah, not offered separately. The layering of offerings on the altar is a feature of temple worship: the perpetual 'olah (the tamid) serves as the fire-base on which all other offerings are placed. The cumulative burning speaks to the multi-dimensional nature of Israel's relationship with YHWH: dedication ('olah), tribute (minchah), and fellowship (shelamim) all ascending together.
6: Zevach shelamim la-YHWH min ha-tzon — from the flock. The shelamim allows both male and female animals; this chapter covers cattle (vv. 1-5), sheep (vv. 6-11), and goats (vv. 12-16). The variety of acceptable animals for the fellowship offering reflects its communal and celebratory character — it is not restricted to the most costly animal. The shared meal it enables is available at different economic levels.
7: Kesev (H3775): 'lamb' (specifically a male sheep). Presented before YHWH at the altar entrance. The lamb presentation begins the sheep variant of the fellowship offering. The gesture of presenting (hiqriv, from qarav) before YHWH at the tent entrance is the same approach-vocabulary used throughout: every sacrifice begins with drawing near.
8: Semicha (laying of hands), slaughter, and blood-tossing are repeated identically for sheep — confirming the structural consistency of the shelamim ritual across animal types. The blood against the altar (al ha-mizbeach saviv) is the same zaraq-gesture as the 'olah. The blood-application at the altar base is the act that formally completes the transfer: the animal's life, represented in its blood, is given to YHWH at the altar.
9: 'alyah (H451): the 'fat tail' — a term specific to the fat-tailed sheep breeds (dumba) common in the ancient Near East, which carry a large deposit of fat in the tail. The fat tail is specified for removal along with the chelev in sheep offerings; it belongs to YHWH's portion just as the internal fat does. The specification of a breed-specific anatomical feature shows the precision of the ritual system — it accounts for the biological realities of the available sacrificial animals.
10: The kidneys and liver lobe are described identically for sheep as for cattle (v. 4), using the same Hebrew terms. This verbatim repetition across animal categories (kelayot veha-yoteret al ha-kaved) reinforces that the theological significance is in the organs themselves — interior, hidden, decision-making organs — rather than in which animal they come from. The consistency of organ-theology across species is deliberate.
11: lechem isheh la-YHWH (lechem, H3899 + isheh, H801): 'food of a fire offering to YHWH.' Isheh is a technical term for what is offered in fire; lechem (bread/food) applied to sacrifice describes what the altar-fire 'feeds' YHWH with — a bold anthropomorphic idiom. The LXX renders it with karpoma (fruit-offering) to avoid the anthropomorphism. The Hebrew text is unashamed to use food language for YHWH's reception of the sacrifice: the covenant God is the host of a meal at which both he and his people eat.
12: 'Ez (H5795): goat (female goat or the species generally). The goat variant of the shelamim is presented with the same formula as the sheep (vv. 7-11). The standardized formula for all three animal variants (cattle, sheep, goat) communicates that the shelamim is a genuinely accessible offering: cattle for the wealthy, sheep for the middle, goats for those with less. The fellowship-meal it enables is not socioeconomically restricted.
13: The goat's semicha, slaughter, and blood-tossing are identical to the prior animal variants. The ritual-formula repetition is characteristic of Leviticus's literary style: the repetition is not editorial redundancy but liturgical specification. The reader (and the officiating priest) must perform each act for each animal exactly as specified; the verbal repetition encodes the requirement that no shortcut is acceptable.
14: The fat covering the entrails (chelev ha-mekaseh et ha-qerev) is specified for the goat offering identically to cattle (v. 3). Chelev is the technically classified 'best fat' — the subcategory of internal organ fat that belongs exclusively to YHWH (v. 16). The exact specification of which fat (not all fat, but the classified chelev) prevents confusion between the dietary law and the offering requirement.
15: The kidneys and liver lobe again — identical in all three animal categories. The threefold repetition of these specific organ designations (vv. 4, 10, 15) establishes them as the fixed 'interior theology' of the shelamim: whatever the animal, the inner most-significant organs go to YHWH. The offerer eats the rest; the priest receives the breast and thigh (Lev 7:28-34); YHWH receives the fat, blood, kidneys, and liver-lobe.
16: Kol chelev la-YHWH: 'all the fat belongs to YHWH.' This concluding declaration for the shelamim chapters is a categorical claim: the best fat of any animal, in any offering context, is YHWH's portion. Reah nihoah closes the offering formula. The chelev-reservation is paired with blood-reservation in v. 17: the two elements most associated with life and fertility in ANE food and religious culture are both entirely YHWH's — they may not be consumed by humans in any context.
17: Chuqat 'olam ledoroteichem (chuqat 'olam, H2708+H5769): 'a perpetual ordinance throughout your generations' — the strongest legal formula in Leviticus, indicating a non-negotiable, permanent prohibition. Chelev and dam (blood) may not be eaten wherever you dwell. The double prohibition (no fat, no blood) applies outside the sacrificial context as well — in any food. The life-blood principle (Lev 17:11) and the best-portion principle (fat to YHWH) together define the outer boundaries of Israelite food ethics: what is most life-containing belongs exclusively to the life-giver.
